coredata.dc:title: On–Off–On fluorescent sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for biothiols detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jiaying
Yang, Junsheng
Ma, Yi
Zhao, Dong
Luo, Huibo
Luo, Xiaogang
Hou, Changjun
Huo, Danqun
coredata.dc:description: Abnormal levels of biothiols in the human body are closely related to a variety of diseases, thus it is crucial for the selective detection of biothiols in the biological system. In this work, a Fe3+-triggered multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on–off-on fluorescent sensing platform for the ultrasensitive detection of biothiols (GSH, Cys and Hcy) was successfully established. In detail, Fe3+ could adsorb on the surface of N-CQDs through coordination with the phenolic hydroxyl group of N-CQDs, resulting in the quenching of the fluorescence of N-CQDsdue to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect from N-CQDs to Fe3+. After the addition of biothiols, there was stronger coordination between Fe3+ and biothiols than N-CQDs-Fe3+, resulting in fluorescence recovery of N-CQDs. On basis of the on–off-on principle, the N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent probe was applied to determine biothiols. Under optimal parameters, the N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent probe exhibited a relatively lower limit of detection (LOD) of 20.39 nM (GSH), 9.31 nM (Hcy) and 1.93 nM (Cys), respectively, which was better or comparable compared with previous reports. Further, the constructed N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent sensing platform was validated with strong anti-interference ability, good reproducibility and repeatability, and also performed well in serum samples, demonstrating a promising potential in clinical diagnosis for biothiols analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Photoinduced electron transfer
Biothiols
Fluorescent sensing platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of biothiols in real sample
Study on the quenching effect of N-CQDs by Fe3+
Feasibility of N-CQDs-Fe3+ for biothiols detection
Determination of biothiols in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 81772290 , 31171684 ), Sichuan Science and Technology Program ( 21ZYZFZDYF0019 ), Chongqing Graduate Student Research Innovation Project (No. CYS22134 ), Wuliangye Group research project ( CXY2020ZR004 ), the Chongqing science and technology commission (CSTC2015shmszxl20097), Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Project, Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University for TEM, XPS, XRD and FT-IR and the sharing fund of Chongqing University's large equipment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and size distribution histogram (B) of N-CQDs; Full scan XPS spectrum of N-CQDs (C), high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (D), O1s (E) and N1s (F); XRD image (G) and FT-IR image (H) of the obtained N-CQDs.

(A) Absorption spectra of the N-CQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths; (C) The comparison of excitation and emission spectra of the as obtained N-CQDs; (D) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs and N-CQDs-Fe3+; (E) The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+; (F) The fluorescence quenching efficiency of Fe3+ to N-CQDs.

The fluorescence spectrum changes when added different concentrations of biothiols (A, D and G); Fitted scatter plot between the changes in fluorescence intensity (ΔF) and biothiols concentrations: GSH (B and C), Hcy (E and F) and Cys (H and I).

The relative fluorescence intensity changes of N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+ and other interferents (A), the relative fluorescence value changes of N-CQDs towards biothiols and other interferents (B) and the repeatability and reproducibility of the prepared sensor in analyzing Cys (C).
Schematic diagram of detecting biothiols based on N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescence sensing platform.
Determination of biothiols in human serum samples by the N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescent sensing platform (n = 3).

head.ce:title: On–Off–On fluorescent sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for biothiols detection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Bio-perception & Intelligent Information Processing, School of Microelectronics and Communication Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A Fe3+-triggered multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots on–off-on fluorescent sensing platform for the ultrasensitive detection of biothiols was successfully established.

Abnormal levels of biothiols in the human body are closely related to a variety of diseases, thus it is crucial for the selective detection of biothiols in the biological system. In this work, a Fe3+-triggered multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on–off-on fluorescent sensing platform for the ultrasensitive detection of biothiols (GSH, Cys and Hcy) was successfully established. In detail, Fe3+ could adsorb on the surface of N-CQDs through coordination with the phenolic hydroxyl group of N-CQDs, resulting in the quenching of the fluorescence of N-CQDsdue to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect from N-CQDs to Fe3+. After the addition of biothiols, there was stronger coordination between Fe3+ and biothiols than N-CQDs-Fe3+, resulting in fluorescence recovery of N-CQDs. On basis of the on–off-on principle, the N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent probe was applied to determine biothiols. Under optimal parameters, the N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent probe exhibited a relatively lower limit of detection (LOD) of 20.39 nM (GSH), 9.31 nM (Hcy) and 1.93 nM (Cys), respectively, which was better or comparable compared with previous reports. Further, the constructed N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent sensing platform was validated with strong anti-interference ability, good reproducibility and repeatability, and also performed well in serum samples, demonstrating a promising potential in clinical diagnosis for biothiols analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biothiols, such as glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy) and cysteine (Cys), are small molecules that play a critical part in sustaining the biological redox homeostasis through the equilibrium of free thiols and disulfides in physiological systems. Moreover, its content in the human body is closely related to a wide range of diseases, such as cancers [1,2], cardiovascular disease [3], and Alzheimer's disease [4], and in some cases, biothiols could be a powerful predictor of mortality. Therefore, it is of great significance for the fast, accurate and sensitive monitoring of those biothiols.

To date, significant efforts have been proposed for biothiols discrimination. Identification methods relied on large instruments [5–7], which led to many defects such as time-consumption, preprocessing and professional operating, although they had good accuracy and sensitivity, thus greatly hindering the applications of the point-of-care test (POCT) and field monitoring. Consequently, it was urgently needed for the construction of a simple-operate, rapid and low-cost sensing platform for biothiols monitoring.

Nowadays, N-CQDs have aroused extensive research interest among researchers ever since their first preparation and subsequently treated as _ fluorescent probes in the field of detection and imaging due to their prominent surface and fluorescence properties. Liao et al. applied N-CQDs for GSH detection_ and obtained a detection limit of 0.05 μM [8]. Also, another research reported that N-CQDs showed a special property in that the fluorescence was quenched by Fe3+ [9]. Among them, there were still many problems that needed to be conquered although favorable sensitivities these sensors obtained. _According to previous studies, Cu2+ complexes could apply to detect biothiols by the principle of indicator replacement analysis (IDA) [10,11]. The principle was mainly based on the coordination of the probe and Cu2+ resulting in fluorescence quenching, and after the addition of biothiols which had _ greater coordination with Cu2+, could separate Cu2+ and probe, leading to fluorescence recovery and achieving biothiols detection. In addition, Zhang et al. constructed a carbon dot-Fe2+ fluorescence sensing system for glucose detection [12]. Glucose oxidase first oxidized glucose to produce H2O2, and then H2O2 oxidized Fe2+ in the system to Fe3+ followed by fluorescence quenching when Fe3+ coordinated with the carbon dots.

Bearing the above-mentioned points in mind, a multifunctional on–off-on N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent sensing platform for the identifying of biothiols (GSH, Cys and Hcy) was constructed. In this work, citrate and ethylenediamine were used as raw materials to synthesize high-quantum yield N-CQDs through hydrothermal treatment. Fe3+ was adsorbed on the surface of N-CQDs by coordination with the phenolic hydroxyl groups, resulting in _ fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, in the presence of GSH, Cys and Hcy, there was _ stronger coordination and greater steric hindrance between GSH, Cys and Hcy and Fe3+, separating N-CQDs from Fe3+, and recovering fluorescence. Based on the on–off-on principle, quantitative determination of biothiols could realize through fluorescence intensity changes. Furthermore, the proposed N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent sensing platform exhibited good sensitivity and anti-interference capability toward biothiols detection and was successfully validated in complex biological samples.

The fluorescent N-CQDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method according to previous research [12]. Specifically, 1.05 g citric acid and 0.34 mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in ultrapure water (20 mL) with ultrasonic treatment. Subsequently, the mixture solution was heated at 200 ℃ for 5 h in a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave. _The obtained dark solution was named as N-CQDs. Next, the prepared N-CQDs solution was transferred to a dialysis bag for 24 h, and transferred to a 50 mL centrifuge tube and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for use. At the same time, part of the above dialyzed N-CQDs solution was centrifuged_ and then subjected to rotary evaporation to obtain a concentrated solution. At last, the concentrated solution was freeze-dried to powder to obtain solid fluorescent carbon quantum dots.

The detection steps of biothiols were as follows: First, 2.4 mL of N-CQDs (dissolved by pH = 7, Tirs-HCl buffer) were added into a 5.0 mL tube with the addition of 300 μL 200 μM Fe3+, and recorded the spectra. Next, the influence of biothiols (GSH, Cys and Hcy) on FL recovery was investigated. In detail, different amounts of analytes were mixed with N-CQDs-Fe3+ solution, shaken well and set for 2.5 min, and measured by a fluorometer LS-55 at the maximum emission peak (excited at 350 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of biothiols in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the capability of the prepared N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent sensing platform to detect biothiols in complex environments, various concentrations of biothiols were spiked into the diluted human normal serum samples (100-fold). And then, tested the fluorescence spectrum of the reaction system under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm and recorded the fluorescence intensities. The recovery rate was obtained by calculation.

The morphological characteristics and structural components of the N-CQDs were systematically performed using TEM, XPS, XRD, Raman, FTIR and UV–vis. TEM was used to characterize and figure out the morphology and dispersion of N-CQDs. N-CQDs exhibited a spherical structure with good monodispersity (Fig. 1
A), and the average particle size distribution diagram of N-CQDs was about 2.6 nm (Fig. 1 B). XPS was applied here to figure out the element composition of the proposed fluorescent probe N-CQDs. The XPS full spectrum of N-CQDs _ exhibited three binding energy peaks located at 284.68 eV (C1s), 399.98 eV (N1s) and 531.38 eV (O1s), respectively (Fig. 1 C). Further, the spectrum of C 1 s mainly contained four peaks, representing CC (284.43 eV), CN/CC (285.79 eV), CO (287.33 eV) and O-CO (288.26 eV), respectively (Fig. 1 D). Fig. 1 E displayed the presence of OH, CO and CO bonds which attributed to the corresponding binding energies 530.76 eV, 531.69 eV and 532.71 eV, respectively. At the same time, it can be inferred that there were NH (398.69 eV), C-NC (399.55 eV) and NC/NN (403.33 eV) bonds in the spectrum of N1s in Fig. 1 F.

Subsequently, it can be seen from the XRD spectrum of N-CQDs in Fig. 1 G, there was a 2θ peak located at 26.5°, which was consistent with the results reported in the literature [13]. Raman spectrum of N-CQDs exhibited two unique D peaks (1340 cm−1) and G peaks (1550 cm−1) of carbon materials ( Figure S1 ), which were mainly due to sp3 disordered carbon atom vibration and the ordered sp2 graphite carbon atom vibration [14]. Further, the FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs ( Fig. 1 H) showed that there were many functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and carbonyl. In detail, the absorption at 3423.15 cm−1 and 2946.62 cm−1 could be related to OH, NH and CH bonds, while the absorption at 1650.43 cm−1 and 1557.91 cm−1 confirmed the existence of − CO, CN and NO bonds. In addition, the absorption peak at 1384.11 cm−1 indicated the characteristic vibration of the –CH3 bond [13], suggesting that the N-CQDs had good hydrophilicity and stability. What’s more, the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of the as-obtained N-CQDs had a strong absorption peak at 345 nm ( Figure S2 ), representing the surface state energy level absorption [15], and the aqueous solution of N-CQDs appeared yellow under sunlight. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of N-CQDs was also estimated and the result was shown in Table S1 . In a word, all the above results verified the successful preparation of the N-CQDs fluorescent probe.

Scheme 1 displayed the working mechanism of biothiols screening by the N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescence sensing platform. In the presence of Fe3+, it can be adsorbed on the surface of N-CQDs through coordination with the phenolic hydroxyl. The outer electronic structure of Fe3+ was 4s23d5, and its d orbital was half full. When the N-CQDs were excited, it was easy to cause the electrons in the excited state to transfer to the 3d orbital in the half-full state in Fe3+, causing the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs [12]. After the addition of biothiols, on the one hand, it will replace the original ligand on N-CQDs due to the stronger coordination ability between the biothiols and Fe3+; On the other hand, it could restore the fluorescence of N-CQDs owing to the greater steric hindrance of the biothiols and increase the distance between N-CQDs and Fe3+, thereby blocking the electron transfer between N-CQDs and Fe3+ [16]. Based on the on–off-on principle, the as-prepared N-CQDs- Fe3+ fluorescent probe can effectively detect biothiols with high sensitivity and high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the quenching effect of N-CQDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Dynamic quenching will affect the excited state of the fluorescent molecule and thus change the fluorescence lifetime without changing the absorption spectrum of the fluorescent molecule. Thus, the absorption spectrum changes of N-CQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ was further explored. Fig. 2
A showed that the absorption spectrum of N-CQDs has not changed significantly, confirming the previous inference [17–19]. Meanwhile, compared with other reported literature for Fe3+ quenched CQDs without doping, it was found that N-CQDs have a higher sensitivity to Fe3+, probably due to the changes in the surface chemistry and electronic structure of the CQDs caused by nitrogen doping, making it easier for Fe3+ to combine with the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs to form a complex, thus having higher sensitivity in detection [16,20].

In addition, the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs under various excitation wavelengths were explored. The fluorescence intensity and fluorescence emission wavelength of N-CQDs will change under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2 B). While emission was at 440 nm, the fluorescence intensity value reached the maximum, indicating that the peak wavelength of the strongest fluorescence emission of the N-CQDs was 440 nm. Therefore, to find out the excitation spectrum of the N-CQDs, scanned the emission spectrum of N-CQDs at 440 nm, and the blue line in _ Fig. 2 C 350 nm was the maximum excitation peak wavelength. Under the best excitation light, the best emission spectrum of the N-CQDs was obtained (Fig. 2 C, red line).

In order to further verify the Fe3+-mediated fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs, the time-correlated single photon counting method (TCSPC) was performed to detect the charge transfer process during the recombination of carbon quantum dots before and after Fe3+ addition. Fig. 2 D displayed the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs before and after adding Fe3+. A single exponential decay model was used for fitting and found that N-CQDs fluorescence lifetime was 12.26 ns before the addition of Fe3+, and shortened to 6.14 ns after the addition of Fe3+, illustrating that the dynamic quenching occurred, resulting in fluorescence quenching after adding Fe3+.

To figure out the optimal concentration of Fe3+ to N-CQDs, different concentrations of Fe3+ were performed to observe the fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence value of N-CQDs gradually decreased as the concentration of Fe3+ changed from 0 to 250 μM (Fig. 2 E). Specifically, when the concentration of Fe3+ reached 200 μM, the fluorescence value began to flatten, and fluorescence intensity reduced by 85.22 % compared with the original one (Fig. 2 F). Therefore, 200 μM Fe3+ was selected as the best concentration for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of N-CQDs-Fe3+ for biothiols detection
ce:section.ce:para:
For the purpose of investigating the feasibility of the prepared N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescence probe for biothiols detection, the fluorescence recovery behavior of the N-CQDs-Fe3+ detection system mediated by biothiols was explored. In Figure S3 , the blue line in each graph was the spectrum of simple N-CQDs, and the red line was the spectrum of N-CQDs with 100 μM GSH ( Figure S3
A), 100 μM Cys ( Figure S3
B) and 100 μM Hcy ( Figure S3
C). There was no significant change in the fluorescence spectrum when added the biothiols into N-CQDs, indicating that biothiols did not affect the fluorescence of N-CQDs. Further, when added 200 μM Fe3+ to the N-CQDs solution, the fluorescence was quenched, demonstrating that the Fe3+ had a good quenching effect. However, the fluorescence recovered after adding 200 μM Fe3+ and 200 μM biothiols to the N-CQDs solution. Consequently, the as-prepared N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescence sensing platform can be successfully applied to biothiols detection.

In order to obtain the optimal conditions, the experimental conditions including temperature, pH and response time of the constructed N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescence sensing platform were optimized. Taking GSH as an example, the influence of temperature on the detection of biothiols was investigated. With the temperature increased (25 ∼ 60 °C), the change value of the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system slowly decreased ( Figure S4
), probably due to the increase of temperature leading to the decline of N-CQDs fluorescence intensity. To make the system more adaptable, 25 °C was chosen for later research. Next, the fluorescence response intensity of N-CQDs-Fe3+ increased first as pH increased from pH = 3 to pH = 7. And when pH was 7, the fluorescence recovery value reached the maximum _and then decreased with the pH further risen ( Figure S5 ). Thus, pH = 7 was chosen for the follow-up experiments. Subsequently, with the addition of GSH ( Figure S6
A), Hcy ( Figure S6
B) and Cys ( Figure S6
C), the fluorescence of the detection system recovered and tended to equilibrium within 2.5 min. Therefore, 2.5 min was carried out in the follow-up research.

Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity changes of N-CQDs at 440 nm after adding different concentrations of GSH, Hcy and Cys were explored. As the concentration of GSH, Hcy and Cys increased, the fluorescence recovered correspondingly. There was a good linear relationship between the GSH concentration (20 to 520 μM) and the fluorescence intensity change value (△F) (R2 = 0.9903), with a linear equation of y = 148.3 + 0.7270 × (Fig. 3
A and 3B) and a calculated detection limit of 20.39 nM; As for Hcy, within the range of Hcy concentration of 1 to 20 μM and 80 to 360 μM, there were two good linear relationships between the concentration of Hcy and △F (R2 = 0.9916 and R2 = 0.9855). The linear equations were y = 0.7803 + 3.176x and y = 152.3 + 0.2610x (Fig. 3 C and 3D), and the calculated detection limit calculated was 9.31 nM; Further, there were also two linear relationships between the concentration of Cys and△F (R2 = 0.9958 and R2 = 0.9901) and the linear equations were y = 0.6132 + 9.306x and y = 250.9 + 0.3596x (Fig. 3 E and 3F), with a relatively lower detection limit of 1.93 nM. Compared with the currently reported biothiols detection methods, the proposed N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescence sensing platform had comparable or better detection limits and wider linear range and could effectively meet the clinical and medical requirements for the sensitivity of biothiols detection ( Table S2 ).

Selectivity is a prerequisite for a sensing platform with good performance. In order to validate the selectivity of the constructed fluorescence sensor_, different metal ions were added to the system to observe the fluorescence intensity changes. As shown in Fig. 4
A, _after the addition of 200 μM Fe3+ and 10-fold Fe3+ concentration of other metal ions, it showed that only Fe3+ can cause obvious quenching of the system, showing that Fe3+ had good specificity for N-CQDs fluorescence quenching. Meanwhile, the selectivity of biothiols detection was investigated by reacting with proteins (BSA, HSA 1 mg/mL), sugars (lactose, glucose, fructose), amino acids (Ala, Gly, Ser, Thr, Trp, IIe, Val, Tyr, Arg, His, Phe, Asp), and ions (K+, Al3+, Na+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Mg2+). Consequently, there were nearly no changes in fluorescence intensity after the addition of 10-fold interfering substances. However, when 200 μM GSH, Hcy and Cys were added the fluorescence intensity recovered significantly (Fig. 4 B), indicating that the constructed N-CQDs -Fe3+ sensing platform had strong specificity for the detection of GSH, Hcy and Cys, and the good anti-interference ability towards other high-concentration interfering substances.

The accuracy of a sensing platform in actual detection applications depends on the repeatability and reproducibility of target detection. Taking Cys as an example, the differences in fluorescence intensity changes in different batches and between different parallel samples of the same batch were examined to explore the repeatability and reproducibility of the N-CQDs-Fe3+ sensing platform. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the N-CQDs-Fe3+ sensing platform for Cys detection in ten different batches and five parallel samples in the same batch were 1.67 % and 4.60 % (Fig. 4 C), respectively, showing that the as-prepared N-CQDs-Fe3+ sensing platform had good repeatability and reproducibility in the Cys detection process and was potential in actual detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of biothiols in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the reliability and practicability of the constructed N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescent sensing platform for detecting GSH, Hcy and Cys in actual samples, the addition of GSH, Hcy, and Cys to human serum was investigated by N-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescence sensing platform. As is shown in Table 1 , the recovery rate was between 88.63 ± 4.32 % and 100.67 ± 1.33 % through measurements, indicating that the N-CQDs-Fe3+ sensing platform had superb recovery rate and reliability, and performed well in a relatively complex environment.

In summary, an N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescent sensing platform that can realize the simple and rapid detection of biothiols (GSH, Hcy and Cys) was successfully constructed. Ultrasensitive detection of biothiols was achieved by the on–off-on principle of competitive binding through N-CQDs, Fe3+ and biothiols. Relatively lower LOD of GSH (20.39 nM), Hcy (9.31 nM), Cys (1.93 nM) was obtained. What’s more, the proposed sensing platform exhibited strong anti-interference ability, good repeatability and reproducibility. Further, it also performed well in actual human serum samples, demonstrating huge potential in biomolecular analysis and clinical application prospects in diagnosis. From proof-of-concept experiments, it was worth noting that the designed N-CQDs-Fe3+ on–off-on fluorescent sensing platform could not only apply for biothiols detection, but also could use for the monitoring of Fe3+ concentrations, showing wide applications.

Jiaying Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Junsheng Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yi Ma: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Dong Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Huibo Luo: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Xiaogang Luo: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis. Changjun Hou: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Danqun Huo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO.81772290, 31171684), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (21ZYZFZDYF0019), Chongqing Graduate Student Research Innovation Project (No. CYS22134), Wuliangye Group research project (CXY2020ZR004), the Chongqing science and technology commission (CSTC2015shmszxl20097), Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Project, Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University for TEM, XPS, XRD and FT-IR and the sharing fund of Chongqing University's large equipment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114595.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-efficiency dynamic sensing of biothiols in cancer cells with a fluorescent beta-cyclodextrin supramolecular assembly
Chem. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.X. Liu, W.L. Zhou, J.J. Li, H.Y. Zhang, X.Y. Dai, Y.H. Liu, Y. Liu, High-efficiency dynamic sensing of biothiols in cancer cells with a fluorescent beta-cyclodextrin supramolecular assembly, Chem Sci, 11 (2020) 4791-4800.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time imaging redox status in biothiols and ferric metabolism of cancer cells in ferroptosis based on switched fluorescence response of gold carbon dots
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.X. Xie, X.Y. Hua, Z.Q. Wang, X.M. Yang, H.W. Huang, Real-Time Imaging Redox Status in Biothiols and Ferric Metabolism of Cancer Cells in Ferroptosis Based on Switched Fluorescence Response of Gold Carbon Dots, Anal Chem, 92 (2020) 11420-11428.
